name: Swahili Coast trunk: Miji ya Pwani → Kilwa → سلطنة زنجبار
description: The Swahili city-states of the East African coast, from the early Miji ya Pwani ('coastal towns', ~800 CE) through the dominance of Kilwa (10th-15th c.), the Portuguese disruption (from 1498), the Omani Sultanate based in Zanzibar (1856-1964), German East Africa (1885-1919), and independence (Tanzania from 1961). This chain captures a 1200-year Swahili continuity across political ruptures.
ethical_notes: The Swahili Coast is central to Indian Ocean world history — a hybrid Bantu-Arab-Persian civilization often marginalized in African AND Arab historiographies. Portuguese arrival (1498) inaugurated 150 years of sacking and monopolization. The 19th-century Omani Zanzibar ran the last major slave market on the western Indian Ocean (closed 1873). This history informs present-day Swahili identity across Tanzania, Kenya, Mozambique, and Comoros.

sources.citation: Horton, M. & Middleton, J. 'The Swahili: The Social Landscape of a Mercantile Society' (2000)
Sheriff, A. 'Dhow Cultures of the Indian Ocean' (2010)

links.description: The 'coastal towns' of the Swahili world — a chain of Bantu-speaking, Islamized trading city-states along the East African littoral. Active from 8th-15th c. CE as nodes in Indian Ocean commerce (Arabia, Persia, India, China).

Sultanate of Kilwa Kisiwani (Tanzania), the dominant Swahili city-state from the 10th-15th c. Minted its own coinage, controlled Sofala gold trade, produced the famous Husuni Kubwa palace complex. Visited by Ibn Battuta (1331). Sacked by Portuguese 1505.

Sultanate of Zanzibar (1856-1964), founded when the Omani empire split in succession dispute; Said bin Sultan had already moved the Omani capital to Zanzibar (1832). Dominated spice and slave trade. British protectorate from 1890. Zanzibar Revolution (12 January 1964) overthrew the Sultan; merger with Tanganyika formed Tanzania (April 1964).
links.ethical_notes: The 1964 Zanzibar Revolution's violence against the Arab and Indian population (~20,000 killed) is a dark episode minimized in Tanzanian national narrative.
